The global deployment of electric vehicles is starting to displace a small but significant amount of oil consumption and the impact is expected to become much more significant by the middle of the next decade. Electric vehicles displaced about 1.7 million barrels per day (b/d) of oil consumption in 2025 up from 0.4 million b/d in 2020, according to the International Energy Agency. Just to add a few thoughts—while I’m still cautious about calling a fundamental bottom in gold, Thursday’s NFP print forced the market to scale back its hyper-aggressive tightening bets.

We could certainly dissect the finer details of the NFP data, like the participation rate, but the tape tends to fixate on the headline payroll figure. The 2s10s curve had been on a relentless flattening path recently, but it shifted into a bull steepening regime right after the report dropped.

The market had tilted so sharply into the 'hawkish Warsh' narrative the short-rate space and the USD became heavily overcrowded. I suspect the upcoming data won't back up those extreme bets, so I’m anticipating a gradual unwinding.

As that unwind plays out, gold, PGMs, and potentially BTC could catch a significant bid. For gold to kick off a clean, beautiful rally, it really needs to be fueled by a USD rollover.

The absolute best case scenario would be the DXY breaking below 100 and building downside momentum. That said i’m fully aware of the risk that if easing US growth expectations coincide with a rollback of tightening bets from foreign central banks like the ECB, the greenback might not flush as hard as people think.

That’s exactly why I’m keeping my guard up. While it would be ideal if the market just rallies from here without giving the weak hands a chance to get back in, I’m holding onto a massive cash cushion to buffer against the risk of another flush.

Because I don't view this as a definitive floor, retail investors trying to play gold via leveraged ETFs are likely setting themselves up for a world of psychological and financial pain.

Frankly, anyone trading 3x leveraged gold or gold miners is out of their mind. In a space this vol, it blows right past what a retail account can realistically survive, meaning they'll likely get stopped out or capitulate long before we ever reach the final destination.

The right playbook here is either using derivatives to build a structured expression or slowly accumulating low-leverage cash equities. At the very least, the r/r ratio has cleared into an highly attractive zone.

As I mentioned in my previous post, outside of gold, my eyes are firmly on Japanese equities. While I'm strictly trading the Nikkei index on a tactical, fast-money basis, I used the June flush to scoop up a few Japanese humanoid names for the core portfolio and plan to sit on them for a while.

What appeals to an old school investor like me is the Japanese humanoid space actually features companies with real numbers printed on their balance sheets.

While humanoids are my high conviction favorite, I’m looking to expand into other sectors within Japan. I haven't spotted the right entry points just yet, so I guess I’ll just pray for a fat finger error flashes the Nikkei down 30% lmao.

What the oil market isn't answering: why are Brent futures near pre-war levels when only 39 ships moved through Hormuz in the last 24 hours. Roughly a 3rd of normal traffic?

Economist Steve Hanke says the market is pricing a fantasy. When oil is genuinely scarce, markets go into backwardation; 

spot prices above futures because people are borrowing from the future to cover today's shortage. That's the normal signal. 

Right now, with inventories near record lows and Hormuz still running at a 3rd of capacity, the forward curve has gone flat instead. Hanke calls that anomalous, plainly disconnected from the physical reality on the water.

Refined products are telling the honest story: gasoline and diesel are still showing real backwardation, meaning real shortages. The crude price is not.

While Brent and WTI crude prices have returned to pre-war levels, refinery margins continue to rise as lower feedstock costs have yet to translate into equally lower fuel prices amid tight product supplies and robust peak-summer demand. In the US, the 3-2-1 crack spread has climbed to its highest level since 2022. The spread is a widely used proxy for refinery profitability, measuring the theoretical margin from processing three barrels of crude into two barrels of gasoline and one barrel of distillate fuel, typically diesel. Its strength highlights a growing disconnect between falling crude prices and still-elevated fuel prices, with strong seasonal demand and constrained product availability supporting refiners’ margins.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 6, 2026

Market Brief

This week, the market focuses on U.S.-Iran talks, China’s June inflation data, and Fed meeting minutes.

A-share trading rules take effect July 6, with ST stock daily limits raised to 10%.

Multiple A-share companies forecast strong first-half earnings growth.

Major OPEC+ producers to raise crude output by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

South Korea plans to establish a future fund with a maximum size of 100 trillion won.

South Korea’s central bank warns that leveraged ETFs tied to Samsung and SK Hynix may amplify market volatility.

Top Stories

Global markets face a busy week. Domestically, China will release June CPI, PPI, and foreign exchange reserve data; A-share trading rules take effect; the 2026 China Internet Conference will be held in Beijing; Luxshare Precision will list on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. Internationally, new U.S.-Iran talks may take place in Pakistan; the U.S. will hold hearings on tariffs imposed on 60 countries; the Fed releases its June meeting minutes; South Korea plans to launch 24-hour foreign exchange trading. Meanwhile, U.S. corporate earnings season kicks off; Apple, OpenAI, and Meta CEOs will gather at the Sun Valley Summit; SpaceX will be added to the Nasdaq-100 Index; SK Hynitz ADR makes its U.S. debut; OpenAI will release GPT-5.6.

After more than two months of technical and operational preparation, new A-share trading rules take effect on July 6. Key changes include: (1) After-hours fixed-price trading will be extended from STAR Market stocks to all A-shares and exchange-traded funds (ETFs); (2) The closing price for ETFs on both exchanges will be determined via closing auction instead of continuous bidding; (3) Daily price limits for ST and *ST stocks on the Shanghai and Shenzhen main boards will be increased from 5% to 10%, aligning with ordinary stocks; (4) The Shenzhen Stock Exchange’s ChiNext board will introduce market makers and optimize block trading mechanisms. Agreement-based block trading confirmation times will be expanded from 15:00–15:30 to 9:30–11:30 and 13:00–15:30.

Seven major OPEC+ oil producers have agreed to increase daily crude output by 188,000 barrels in August, reaffirming their commitment to market stability. This marks the fifth consecutive month of output hikes since the outbreak of the U.S.-Iran conflict, with cumulative increases totaling 940,000 barrels per day—equivalent to nearly 1% of global demand.

South Korea’s central bank has warned that leveraged exchange-traded funds (ETFs) tracking single stocks—specifically Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix—could exacerbate market concentration, amplify volatility, and generate one-sided capital flows. If investment in these single-stock leveraged ETFs continues to expand, market centralization risks will worsen. The central bank cautioned that retail investor losses could be magnified by leverage during a market correction and plans to strengthen monitoring of these ETFs’ impact on financial stability.

China Macro & Policy

Vice Premier Zhang Guoqing of the State Council conducted a field inspection in Xinjiang from July 2 to 5, reviewing central enterprises’ support for local industrial development and employment. Zhang emphasized aligning industrial investment with Xinjiang’s resource endowments and development plans, promoting sustainable, job-creating projects, and participating in infrastructure construction including transportation, power, telecommunications, oil and gas supply, and industrial and municipal water systems.

China’s Ministry of Commerce stated that technical consultations on a China-UK services trade agreement feasibility study concluded in London on June 30–July 1, achieving substantive progress. Both sides agreed to accelerate follow-up work to complete the joint feasibility study as soon as possible.

China Equities Markets

CITIC Securities noted that the K-shaped divergence in the A-share market, driven by narrative shifts, has recently shown signs of volatility and convergence—normal market behavior. It expects significant relief in outflows from broad-market ETFs, representing the most important marginal shift in market liquidity. Corrections to the interest rate narrative, combined with improved liquidity conditions, may catalyze a rebound in non-AI sectors with solid earnings fundamentals. The firm continues to recommend an AI plus energy and chemicals allocation strategy.

As of the first half of 2026, Hong Kong-listed companies have repurchased shares totaling over 90 billion Hong Kong dollars, involving more than 270 firms—among the highest levels in recent years. June saw the highest monthly figures for both number of repurchasing companies and total amount. Technology and internet firms remain the primary drivers of Hong Kong share buybacks.

On July 5, several A-share firms including China Merchants Energy Shipping and Dongfang Shenghong forecast first-half earnings growth of onefold or more, spanning shipping, chemicals, and optical communications. The first half of 2026 saw cyclical recoveries in petrochemicals and non-ferrous metals, driving strong profit growth among industry leaders.

Zhongji Xuchuang stated that market rumors claiming its upstream material, “Xuanguang sheets,” were blocked are untrue. The company maintains normal procurement channels, and suppliers are actively supporting operations with no supply disruptions.

Hon Hai reported June revenue of 821.763 billion new Taiwan dollars, up 52.11% year-over-year; second-quarter revenue reached 2.51 trillion new Taiwan dollars, up 39.83% year-over-year. Hon Hai expects continued growth in AI rack shipments in the third quarter, with overall operational performance forecast to rise both sequentially and year-over-year.

China Industry News

China’s logistics performance index for June stood at 50.6%, up 0.3 percentage points from May. Manufacturing logistics demand remained steady, consumer logistics demand held firm, and holiday spending boosted wholesale, retail, and supermarket operations.

The “Beijing Digital Economy Development Report (2025–2026)” blue book showed Beijing’s digital economy output surpassed 2.4 trillion yuan in 2025, growing 8.7% year-over-year, accounting for 46.4% of GDP. Beijing ranked second globally in the Digital Economy Benchmark City Index with a score of 0.770, maintaining its status as a “global leading city.”

Air China, China Eastern Airlines, and China Southern Airlines confirmed that domestic airfare fuel surcharges will be reduced effective July 5. The new rates are 50 yuan per segment for flights under 800 kilometers and 100 yuan per segment for flights over 800 kilometers—down 30 yuan and 50 yuan respectively from previous levels.

The China (Nanjing) Embodied Intelligence Robotics Industry Exhibition will be held from July 10 to 12, expected to attract over 450 domestic and international exhibitors and 35,000 professional visitors.

The digital terminal market is undergoing prolonged structural turbulence. Multiple PC dealers in Shanghai reported that component and finished product prices are already at extreme highs, with further increases anticipated and no sign of relief for at least a year. The DIY market and component pricing are experiencing daily volatility.

Prices in the Huaqiangbei storage market have rebounded, with memory modules and solid-state drives rising. Kingston 16GB DDR4 memory modules, priced at 680 yuan in mid-May, now range from 750 to 800 yuan, returning to the 700–800 yuan range seen in March.

According to online platform data, total box office revenue for the 2026 summer movie season (including presales) has exceeded 2 billion yuan.

Fintech

Artificial intelligence company Anthropic plans to procure at least 1.4 gigawatts of data center computing power in Australia, corresponding to approximately $15 billion in construction costs—far exceeding prior market expectations. Anthropic aims to have at least 1 gigawatt operational by the end of 2027 and has established an office in Australia to advance local infrastructure deployment.

Corporate News & Updates

Uber is reportedly pausing expansion of its European food delivery business as it continues efforts to acquire its largest regional competitor. Uber is still pursuing a takeover of Germany’s Delivery Hero, whose board rejected a €10 billion offer in May.

Kawasaki Heavy Industries and Toyota are independently developing hydrogen-fueled generators and hydrogen-powered sports cars, positioning hydrogen internal combustion engines as a lower-cost alternative to fuel cells.

Global Macro & Policy

South Korea plans to establish a future fund using excess tax revenues from the semiconductor boom to invest in growth engines, support youth, and address rising social inequality. The fund could reach up to 100 trillion won and is expected to complete tax collection by early 2027.

Iran’s commercial counselor in Qatar stated that maritime trade between Iran’s Bandar-e-Deyr port and Qatar’s Al Ruwais port has resumed after a five-month interruption, following coordination with Qatari authorities.

Russian presidential spokesperson Peskov said Russia remains open to peace negotiations and expects the U.S. to play a mediating role, welcoming visits from U.S. envoy Witkoff and Trump’s son-in-law Kushner. Russia continues to maintain contact with the U.S. through existing channels despite their prolonged absence.

Germany plans to raise its 2027 net new borrowing to 118 billion euros, a 7% increase from April projections. The adjustment reflects weaker-than-expected tax revenues, higher debt servicing costs due to rising interest rates, and additional funding needs for the federal employment agency responsible for unemployment benefits.

Global Equity Markets

Goldman Sachs’ high-beta momentum basket fell 18% over two trading sessions—the largest drop since 2020. The strategy has experienced multiple two-day declines exceeding 10% followed by recoveries in 2026, and the current pattern is highly similar. Goldman previously warned of a July correction and believes the drop has created room for tactical rebounds, but extreme positioning means further deleveraging could lead to a maximum drawdown twice the current level.

Driven by rising equity markets, Korean brokerage firms recorded record-high financing volumes in the second quarter. According to the Korea Financial Investment Association, daily average balances of margin loans and stock pledge loans by retail investors reached 61.98 trillion won in Q2, up from the prior quarter.

Bonds

The final ruling in the Dalian Machine Tool fraud bond case sentenced two individuals to five and six years in prison. Legal experts noted that the company’s historical liabilities were extinguished through bankruptcy restructuring, and the 20 million yuan criminal fine was waived. Investors holding the original bonds have had their claims settled in the restructuring process and cannot seek new remedies based on this criminal verdict.

As of March 31, 2026, the total U.S. public debt reached $39.07 trillion. The U.S. Government Accountability Office forecasts that under current policy, public debt will grow more than twice as fast as economic output, reaching 123% of GDP by 2036. More alarming is the cost of servicing debt: the Congressional Budget Office projects interest payments will exceed $1 trillion for the first time in 2026 and reach $2.1 trillion by 2036. The U.S. federal government is on an “unsustainable fiscal path.”

Commodities

China’s commodity price index for June stood at 130.3, down 1.7% month-over-month but up 17.6% year-over-year. Despite a decline influenced by multiple macro factors, domestic and international demand is gradually recovering, and the commodity market remains stable with strong resilience. Experts expect the commodity market to maintain a steady upward trend in the second half of the year.

On July 5, the number of vessels transiting the Oman coastal route through the Strait of Hormuz sharply declined. The previous day, multiple vessels departing the strait suddenly reversed course, highlighting Iran’s continued control over this strategic waterway. Iran has repeatedly stated that ships must transit the strait only through designated and authorized Iranian channels. Data showed that on July 4, 19 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz in both directions, but only one publicly indicated it would follow the Oman coastal route.

FX & Currency Markets

CICC noted that after the weaker-than-expected June nonfarm payrolls data, prior U.S. dollar long positions may have taken profits. However, a sustained dollar decline will likely require further data weakness before breaking below the uptrend established since May. Market volatility may ease as the traditional summer holiday season begins in Europe and the U.S.

AI Leads US Job Cuts for Record 4th Month as Tech Claims 31% of H1 Layoffs | Mark Rutherford, Techtimes

Artificial intelligence has become the leading stated reason for US job cuts for four consecutive months — a streak with no precedent in outplacement data — and the technology sector has absorbed nearly a third of all US layoff announcements in the first half of 2026, according to a report released Wednesday by outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas. Between January and June, US tech employers announced 139,156 job cuts — an 83% surge from the 76,214 recorded in the same period of 2025 — while AI was explicitly cited in 101,743 of all US layoff announcements this year, representing approximately 23% of every cut tracked by Challenger, Gray & Christmas across the entire economy.

The back-to-back-to-back-to-back months in which AI ranked as the leading stated reason for layoffs marks a structural shift that Challenger's own data — which began tracking AI as a distinct cut reason in 2023 — has not recorded before. "Tech remains the epicenter of this year's cuts," said Andy Challenger, chief revenue officer at the Chicago-based firm. "AI is the dominant force as companies are restructuring around it, automating roles, and reallocating budgets toward new capabilities. The sector is being reshaped in real time."

But the firms driving that streak are, in almost every case, the same firms posting their strongest-ever financial results — a paradox that has drawn escalating scrutiny from economists, labor researchers, and the workers on the receiving end of the cuts.

What Tech's 31% Share of US Layoffs Actually Means for Workers

The headline June numbers offered some surface-level relief. US employers across all sectors announced 45,849 job cuts during the month — the lowest monthly total since December 2025, down 53% from May's 97,006. Tech specifically saw 15,503 cuts in June, down sharply from May's 38,242, which had been the sector's highest single-month total since August 2024.

Challenger attributed the June moderation to seasonal patterns rather than any underlying change in direction. "The pace of layoffs cooled considerably in June, similar to plans last June, and as is typical for summer months," he said. "That said, the cuts we are seeing remain concentrated in technology, and artificial intelligence continues to reshape how companies think about headcount."

The full first-half picture reinforces that reading. Total US layoff announcements reached 443,604 through June — down 40% from the government-distorted 744,308 recorded in H1 2025, when mass federal workforce reductions drove that period's total to historic highs. Adjusting for that distortion, 2026 is running roughly in line with 2024. What has fundamentally changed is where the cuts are falling: tech's share has ballooned to nearly a third of the remaining total, a concentration last seen during the post-pandemic correction of 2022-2023. In the second quarter alone, employers announced 226,242 cuts, up 4% from Q1's 217,362 — signaling that the year's structural trend is not abating even as monthly figures bounce seasonally.

Profitable Companies Cut While Committing Record Capex

The Challenger data crystallizes what analysts have called the defining paradox of 2026's technology labor market: the same companies eliminating tens of thousands of roles are simultaneously committing to the largest capital investment cycle in the industry's history.

Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta have collectively guided 2026 capital expenditure to an estimated $700 billion — nearly double their combined 2025 actual spend — with the bulk directed at AI data centers, custom silicon, and inference infrastructure. Meta reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $56.3 billion, up 33% year over year, then executed 8,000 layoffs in May while raising its 2026 capex guidance to $115-145 billion. Google Cloud revenue grew 63% year over year in Q1 2026. Microsoft reported revenue of $82.9 billion and operating income of $38.4 billion in its fiscal third quarter of 2026, up 18% and 20% year over year respectively.

The financial logic driving simultaneous cuts and capex is visible in corporate filings. Meta's $115-145 billion infrastructure spending envelope dwarfs its human payroll budget — meaning the layoffs function more as a financing mechanism than as a direct cost-saving exercise. Cisco provides the year's starkest illustration: on May 14, the San Jose networking company notified roughly 4,000 workers of their elimination on the same day it reported record quarterly revenue of $15.8 billion, a 12% year-on-year gain. CFO Mark Patterson told analysts that the restructuring was "not a savings-driven" exercise but a rapid reallocation toward silicon, optics, security, and AI.

"Regardless of whether individual jobs are being replaced by AI, the money for those roles is," Challenger said in an earlier report this year.

Is AI Washing Inflating the Numbers? What Economists Say

The Challenger methodology records the reason companies themselves report for their cuts — it does not independently verify causation. That distinction has generated sustained scrutiny from economists and, unusually, from some of the technology industry's own leaders.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman acknowledged the gap in February and again in March, saying: "Almost every company that does layoffs is blaming AI, whether or not it really is about AI." His framing — which he labeled "AI washing" — captures a dynamic Deutsche Bank analysts had predicted in January: "AI redundancy washing will be a significant feature of 2026."

The critics are specific about the mechanism. Marc Andreessen argued that companies are using AI as a "silver bullet excuse" for correcting pandemic-era overhiring — which he estimated left many large tech firms overstaffed by 25% to 75%. Wharton management professor Peter Cappelli put the same point more bluntly: "The headline is, 'It's because of AI,' but if you read what they actually say, they say, 'We expect that AI will cover this work.' Hadn't done it. They're just hoping." Oxford Economics concluded in January that firms "don't appear to be replacing workers with AI on a significant scale."

Even Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang called the practice "lazy," saying it did not make business sense that companies were replacing workers at the current scale through AI systems. Tim Sweeney, CEO of Epic Games, broke with the prevailing corporate script explicitly when announcing more than 1,000 cuts: "Since it's a thing now, I should note that the layoffs aren't related to AI."

EY-Parthenon chief economist Greg Daco offered a more measured reading. "We are seeing a lot of layoff announcements that are supposedly related to greater use of AI," he said. "They are aimed at cutting down on labor expenses, while AI investment is growing very rapidly, but I'm not entirely sure this is a replacement situation where talent is being one-for-one substituted."

What is not in dispute is the budget mechanism. Challenger's Andy Challenger described it plainly: companies are "shifting budgets toward AI investments at the expense of jobs." Not direct replacement — budget reallocation. The distinction matters for workers who are told their roles were automated but whose replacement is a GPU cluster, not a chatbot doing their job.

A Gartner survey of 350 executives at companies with revenue above $1 billion — all of them already deploying AI — found that roughly 80% had reduced headcount. But Gartner analyst Helen Poitevin identified a critical flaw in the premise: "Workforce reductions may create budget room, but they do not create return. Organizations that improve ROI are not those that eliminate the need for people, but those that amplify them." The survey found no meaningful difference in financial returns between companies making the deepest cuts and those cutting the least.

What Happens When AI Absorbs Junior Developer Work

Whatever the precise attribution, the structural effects are measurable and concentrated in a specific cohort. Stanford HAI's 2026 AI Index found that employment for software developers between the ages of 22 and 25 fell nearly 20% from 2024 levels — even as headcount for developers over 26 continued to grow.

The mechanism is architectural. AI tools — code-generation assistants, scripted testing platforms, automated bug-fix pipelines — now perform the boilerplate and routine coding that junior roles were historically built around. Senior engineers, equipped with those tools, can handle the same throughput without a junior team beneath them. The junior role is not being automated directly; it is being compressed out of existence because the work that defined it no longer requires a separate human to execute.

This creates a consequence the data does not yet show but that the talent pipeline structure makes likely: if companies stop hiring junior engineers at scale, they stop producing mid-level and senior engineers at scale. The experience ladder — entry-level to mid-level to principal — runs through the roles that are disappearing. One-third of surveyed organizations in the Stanford report anticipated workforce reductions due to AI in the coming year, with expected cuts highest in software engineering, service operations, and supply chain.

The human cost is already accumulating. A Goldman Sachs analysis published in April 2026 estimated that AI is eliminating roughly 25,000 US jobs per month while creating approximately 9,000 new ones — a net monthly reduction of 16,000. Reemployment timelines for laid-off tech workers are stretching, reflecting both the volume of displaced workers entering the market and a widening mismatch between eliminated roles and available positions.

Ken Matos, an organizational psychologist and director of insights at hiring platform HiBob, expects hiring to rebound after the current investment cycle matures. "Right now, companies are moving labor dollars into tech investment," he said. "Hopefully, that moves back into labor dollars once the technology is set up." But he was direct about who would benefit: "The people who get laid off don't necessarily get the next set of jobs, because the roles are different."

Does the AI Layoff Explanation Hold Up Without a Law to Test It?

One reason the AI washing debate cannot be resolved from the outside is structural: no federal law currently requires employers to disclose whether AI played a material role in a mass layoff.

The federal WARN Act — enacted in 1988, requiring 60 days' advance notice for mass layoffs at companies with 100 or more employees — mandates notice but imposes no obligation to state a reason. A company can publicly attribute thousands of layoffs to AI, and affected workers have no legal right to verify or contest that explanation.

A pending federal bill, the AI Workforce PREPARE Act, would amend the WARN Act to require employers to disclose when AI was a "substantial factor" in a mass layoff, name the AI systems used, estimate the percentage of job losses attributable to them, and describe any retraining steps taken before cuts were made. The bill has not passed.

At the state level, California has moved furthest and fastest. In May, Governor Gavin Newsom signed Executive Order N-6-26, directing the California Labor and Workforce Development Agency to undertake a 180-day review of potential revisions to the state's Cal-WARN Act — including shorter trigger thresholds and new AI-specific disclosure requirements. California also launched what state officials described as the nation's first public dashboard tracking potential AI-related job displacement, using unemployment insurance data to identify early indicators by occupation. Initial data from the tracker showed no evidence of rising statewide unemployment claims in AI-exposed occupations overall, though it did identify impacts on workers in high-exposure positions.

California's SB 951 — which would require 90 days' advance written notice before AI-driven layoffs affecting 25 or more workers — has stalled in the state Assembly. A federal companion bill, the No Robot Bosses Act, which would require human oversight when AI tools are used in employment decisions, remains pending in Congress. Similar disclosure bills have been introduced in at least seven states this legislative session, but none have become law. Colorado's original AI governance law, which had been scheduled to take effect June 30, 2026, was replaced by revised legislation before it took effect; the replacement narrows the state's framework to a disclosure-only model and takes effect January 1, 2027, with enforcement left to the state Attorney General and no private right of action.

RAISE US and the Retraining Gap

On June 25 — eight days before this report — former Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo and former Indiana Governor Eric Holcomb launched RAISE US, a nonpartisan nonprofit backed by Amazon, Anthropic, Microsoft, and the OpenAI Foundation, along with more than two dozen corporate and philanthropic partners. The coalition has raised more than $500 million toward a $1 billion multi-year goal, with initial pilot programs in Arkansas, Connecticut, Maryland, and Utah.

The initiative carries an embedded irony that Raimondo acknowledged directly: the companies building the technology that employers cite when cutting those jobs are writing the first checks to retrain the workers displaced by those cuts. "America has a technology strategy for leading the global AI competition," she said at the launch. "It does not yet have a people strategy, and we cannot lead without one."

Raimondo herself called past federal retraining efforts "ineffective" — a candid assessment that doubles as the central challenge RAISE US must answer. The historical record is not reassuring. A 2025 Brookings Institution analysis reviewed six decades of US federal retraining programs and found that randomized controlled trials showed no statistically significant improvement in employment or earnings for participants. An arXiv study published in May 2026 analyzed more than 23 million participation records from the federal Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act between 2017 and 2023, finding that retraining rarely moved workers into less automation-exposed jobs.

The RAISE US advisory board draws on serious labor economists — MIT's David Autor, Harvard's Raj Chetty, Erik Brynjolfsson — and includes AFL-CIO President Liz Shuler, a deliberate signal of labor-table inclusion. Whether $500 million in privately funded pilots can produce outcomes that six decades of federal programs did not is the question the next three years will answer.

What the Next Four Years Will Reveal

Economists broadly caution against projecting the current trend forward without accounting for the investment cycle it is embedded in. The $700 billion committed by the four largest hyperscalers in 2026 must begin generating sufficient revenue to justify its own depreciation burden — a reckoning that most forecasters place in the 2027-2030 window. If AI-driven productivity gains materialize at the scale companies are betting on, the revenue base expands and some displaced roles eventually return in reconfigured form. If they do not, the workforce reduction will have preceded the productivity gain, leaving both workers and investors worse off.

What is not speculative is the concentration of capital involved. The four hyperscalers are now committing more to data centers annually than ExxonMobil, Chevron, Shell, and BP combined spend on oil and gas exploration. BCG has projected that up to 15% of US jobs could face elimination over the next five years if AI adoption broadens beyond the technology sector — though that scenario, cited in an earlier report, remains unconfirmed at scale and is contested by economists including Daron Acemoğlu, who argues humans will remain necessary and complementary to AI rather than replaced by it.

"Right now, companies are moving labor dollars into tech investment," Matos said. "Hopefully, that moves back into labor dollars once the technology is set up."

For any worker in an AI-exposed role, the question is whether the transition window — during which payroll budgets have shifted to GPU procurement but AI-generated productivity revenue has not yet materialized — is short enough to survive. The H1 2026 Challenger data suggests the window is open and widening. Whether RAISE US, stalled disclosure bills, or eventually a labor market rebound closes it before the next generation of engineers fails to enter the pipeline is what 2026 through 2030 will determine. For workers deciding now whether to stay in an AI-exposed role, upskill toward AI-adjacent work, or wait for the legislative backstop that has not yet arrived, the H1 data offers a clear signal: the restructuring is accelerating, the policy response is thin, and the companies funding the only retraining initiative on the table are the same ones executing the cuts.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are companies really cutting jobs because of AI, or is that a cover story?

Both, according to named economists and company executives. Outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas records whatever reason companies themselves report — no independent verification of causation is required. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman acknowledged in public remarks that some AI attribution is genuine displacement while some is what he called "AI washing": citing AI for cuts the company would have made anyway. Oxford Economics concluded in January 2026 that firms "don't appear to be replacing workers with AI on a significant scale." The clearest documented pattern is budget reallocation: payroll savings from workforce reductions are being redirected to AI infrastructure investment. Challenger's own framing captures this directly — "the money for those roles is" being replaced by AI spending, whether or not the specific task was automated.

Why are profitable companies cutting jobs while spending hundreds of billions on AI?

The financial logic is a capital reallocation story rather than a cost-cutting story. Meta, for example, reported $56.3 billion in first-quarter 2026 revenue — up 33% year over year — while simultaneously executing layoffs and guiding its 2026 capital expenditure to $115-145 billion, mostly for AI infrastructure. The workforce reduction frees up operating cash that is then redirected to infrastructure whose depreciation costs will run for years. Cisco cut 4,000 workers on the day it reported record quarterly revenue of $15.8 billion; its CFO called the restructuring "not savings-driven." A Gartner survey of 350 enterprises found that companies making the deepest cuts showed no improvement in financial returns over those cutting the least — suggesting the layoffs are funding a bet on future AI returns that has not yet materialized.

What federal law protects workers from AI-attributed layoffs, and what are its gaps?

The federal WARN Act (1988) requires 60 days' advance notice before mass layoffs at companies with 100 or more employees — but it does not require employers to state any reason for the layoffs. A company can publicly cite AI as the cause and workers have no legal right to verify or contest that explanation. The pending AI Workforce PREPARE Act would amend WARN to require AI-reason disclosure, including naming the specific systems used and estimating the percentage of cuts attributable to them — but the bill has not passed. California's SB 951, which would require 90 days' notice for AI-driven layoffs, is stalled in the state Assembly. As of now, the federal disclosure gap means that Challenger's AI-cited figures rest entirely on voluntary, unverified corporate self-reporting.

If AI is eliminating junior developer roles, what happens to the next generation of senior engineers?

This is the structural consequence the current numbers do not yet show but the talent pipeline makes likely. Stanford HAI's 2026 AI Index documented a nearly 20% drop in employment for software developers ages 22-25, while headcount for older developers grew. The mechanism is task compression: AI code-generation and testing tools let senior engineers absorb the boilerplate work that junior roles were built around, so companies stop hiring juniors rather than replacing them directly. The consequence is a pipeline gap: if organizations stop training junior engineers at scale in 2026, the cohort that would have become mid-level and senior engineers in 2028-2030 simply does not exist. Gartner forecasts that half of companies that attributed headcount reductions to AI will rehire staff in similar functions under different titles by 2027 — but whether those rehires will come from a junior pipeline that no longer exists is the open question that the H1 2026 data cannot yet answer.

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

MARKET UPDATE — MONDAY, JULY 6, 2026

Markets are starting the week with a strange mix:

🔸 tech futures are bouncing
🔸 oil is falling
🔸 bond yields remain stubborn
🔸 and the Japanese yen is flashing risk warnings again

This is not a simple “risk-on” day.
It is a market trying to celebrate lower inflation pressure while quietly asking whether the AI trade has become too crowded.

🟢 MARKETS: TECH LEADS THE REBOUND

US futures are higher after the July 4 holiday weekend.

S&P 500 futures: around +0.4%
Nasdaq 100 futures: around +1.1%
Dow futures: broadly flat
VIX: around 16.3

The Nasdaq is leading because chip stocks are rebounding after last week’s sharp sell-off.

Intel and Micron rose premarket after heavy losses in semiconductors last Thursday.

Simple explanation:
When chip stocks rise, the Nasdaq usually rises because AI and semiconductor companies are now some of the biggest engines of the US stock market.

But this rebound needs confirmation.
The semiconductor sector has already had a huge run this year, and investors are now asking whether future profits can justify the prices.

🟢 BONDS & FED: THE MARKET STILL FEARS HIGHER RATES

Treasury yields are slightly lower this morning:

1-year yield: around 3.94%
2-year yield: around 4.11%
10-year yield: around 4.46%
30-year yield: around 4.98%

The key point is this:
Yields are not collapsing, even though oil has fallen and jobs data recently weakened.
That matters.

Simple explanation:

Bond yields are like the interest rate the US government has to pay to borrow money.

When yields stay high, it tells us investors still think inflation or tight Fed policy may remain a problem.

The market is now waiting for Wednesday’s Fed minutes from the first meeting under Chair Kevin Warsh.

Traders want to know whether the Fed is preparing to stay tough on inflation — or whether weaker jobs and cheaper oil will reduce pressure for another rate hike.

This week also brings a 10-year Treasury auction on Wednesday and a 30-year auction on Thursday.

That matters because the stock market is trying to rally while bond yields remain stubbornly high.

Simple explanation:

If investors demand higher interest to lend money, borrowing stays expensive — and expensive money can pressure stocks, especially long-duration technology shares.

🟢 OIL: FROM HORMUZ PANIC TO SUPPLY GLUT FEARS

Oil continues to fall.

WTI crude: around $68–69
Brent crude: around $71–72

OPEC+ has agreed to raise production by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

That is the fifth consecutive monthly increase.

This comes as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has improved after the US-Iran 60-day ceasefire.

Weekly crude oil tanker crossings have rebounded to about 57 (up from a wartime average of 15). However, this is still significantly lower than pre-war baseline, which was was roughly 230 to 240 crude oil tanker crossings per week.

Simple explanation:

A few months ago, markets feared the world might not get enough oil.

Now the worry is moving in the opposite direction: too much supply, weaker demand, and lower prices.

That is why some banks now see Brent potentially falling towards $60–65 by year-end if Hormuz keeps reopening and supply continues to return.

For ordinary people, cheaper oil can mean lower petrol and transport costs.

For markets, cheaper oil can reduce inflation pressure.
For energy stocks, however, lower oil can hurt profits.

🟢 AI & TECH: A HUGE WEEK FOR THE AI TRADE

This week could test investor appetite for AI again.

Three major events matter:

🔸Samsung is expected to give a second-quarter update tomorrow.

🔸SK Hynix is preparing an approximately $28bn US listing, one of the largest share sales in history.

🔸SpaceX officially joins the Nasdaq-100 tomorrow.

South Korea is also becoming a warning sign for AI-related speculation.

The KOSPI has surged dramatically over the past year, but volatility has been extreme and foreign investors have been pulling money out.

That matters because Samsung and SK Hynix are now central to the global AI supply chain.

SpaceX’s inclusion matters because funds tracking the Nasdaq-100 may need to buy the stock automatically.

Simple explanation:

When a company joins a major index, some funds have to buy it because their job is to copy that index.

That can create short-term demand.

But it does not guarantee long-term gains.

The real question is still whether earnings, margins and cash flows can support today’s valuations.

🟢 THE EARNINGS BUBBLE QUESTION

The next major test is earnings season.

Investors will be watching whether AI spending by big technology companies is producing enough revenue growth to justify today’s valuations.

In simple terms:

The market has already priced in a lot of good news. Now companies need to prove the profits are real.

This is the biggest quiet risk beneath the surface.

Wall Street analysts now expect very strong S&P 500 profit growth, helped by AI, chips and resilient consumers.

But some investors worry expectations have risen too fast.

The danger is not that AI is fake.

The danger is that markets may be pricing AI profits as if everything goes perfectly.

Simple explanation:

Imagine a student who is expected to score 99% in every exam.

Even if they score 90%, that may still be excellent — but markets may treat it as disappointment.

That is the risk with AI stocks now.

Great companies can still fall if expectations become unrealistic.

🟢 SMALL CAPS: A CONTRARIAN SIGNAL

The Russell 2000 has had a strong year, but investor flows into small-cap funds remain low by historical standards.

That is interesting because neglected areas of the market can sometimes become attractive when too much attention is focussed on mega-cap tech.

Simple explanation:

If everyone is crowded into the same few giant companies, smaller companies can be ignored.

Sometimes that creates opportunity.

But small caps usually need falling rates, stable growth and easier credit conditions to perform well.

🟢 CURRENCIES: THE YEN WARNING RETURNS

The dollar is stronger today.

The yen remains weak, with Goldman Sachs reportedly raising its 12-month USD/JPY forecast to 165.

This matters because a weak yen can encourage the carry trade.

That means investors borrow cheaply in yen and invest in higher-returning global assets.

Simple explanation:

It is like borrowing from a friend who charges almost no interest, then using that money to chase bigger returns elsewhere.

It feels easy while the yen stays weak.

But if the yen suddenly strengthens, traders may have to unwind those positions quickly.

That can force selling across stocks, bonds and other risk assets.

Japan may intervene again if the yen weakens too far, but intervention usually gives temporary relief unless the interest-rate gap changes.

🟢 CORPORATE DEAL FLOW

Corporate activity is also picking up.

🔸Comcast’s Sky is buying ITV’s TV business in a deal reported around the 🔸–$2bn range.

🔸EasyJet shares rose after a takeover approach from Castlelake.

These are not the main market stories, but they show that dealmaking is still alive despite high rates and uncertain growth.

🟢 STOCKS TO WATCH TODAY

Several individual stocks are also in focus:

🔸 Intel and Micron — rising premarket as chip stocks try to rebound after last week’s sharp sell-off.

🔸 Strategy / MSTR — higher premarket as Bitcoin-linked stocks continue to attract attention.

🔸 easyJet — jumped in London after a takeover approach from Castlelake.

🔸 Comcast / ITV — Comcast’s Sky is buying ITV’s TV business in a deal reported around $1.6bn–$2bn.

Simple explanation:

These are not the main market driver, but they show where traders may see extra movement today.

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS

Today’s market is being pulled in two directions.

Bullish side:

🔸 Oil is falling
🔸 Inflation pressure may ease
🔸 Tech is rebounding
🔸 AI demand remains powerful
🔸 Earnings expectations are strong

Bearish side:

🔸 Bond yields remain high
🔸 Fed minutes could sound hawkish
🔸 AI expectations may be too stretched
🔸 The yen carry trade is a hidden risk
🔸 Oil’s fall could also signal weaker demand

The market is fragile.

The main risk is that investors are assuming three things at once:

🔸inflation falls

🔸earnings surge

🔸AI keeps delivering

If all three happen, the rally can continue.

If one fails, volatility may return quickly.

Thought for the Day:
“The market does not punish optimism. It punishes optimism without a margin of safety.”

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

text:
🔥 **สัปดาห์ระเบิดข้อมูลเศรษฐกิจ!** Fed Minutes • PMI บริการ • ADP จ้างงาน • Jobless Claims • บ้านมือสอง + IEA Oil Report ครบเซ็ต 6-10 ก.ค. 2026  
หนึ่งสัปดาห์ก่อน **Earnings Season** ระเบิด… ตลาด Forex หุ้น ทองคำ น้ำมัน จะผันผวนแค่ไหน? วิเคราะห์เจาะลึก + สัญญาณสำคัญที่นี่! 💥

---

**เนื้อหาข่าวเต็ม (พร้อมวิเคราะห์เจาะลึก) – คัดลอกไปใช้ได้เลย:**

พรุ่งนี้ (จันทร์ที่ 6 ก.ค.) สัปดาห์การเงินสหรัฐฯ จะเริ่มต้นด้วยข้อมูลสำคัญที่นักลงทุนและเทรดเดอร์ทั่วโลกจับตาแบบไม่กะพริบตา! เพราะนี่คือ “Data Heavy Week” ก่อนที่ **Earnings Season** ของบริษัทมหาชนจะเริ่มต้นอย่างเป็นทางการในอีกประมาณ 7 วันข้างหน้า

**จันทร์ 6 ก.ค. – June S&P Global Services PMI (ข้อมูลเบื้องต้น/Flash)**  
ภาคบริการของสหรัฐฯ ซึ่งคิดเป็นกว่า 70% ของเศรษฐกิจ ขยายตัวดีขึ้นเป็น 51.3 ในเดือนมิถุนายน (จาก 50.7 เดือนพฤษภาคม) สูงกว่าคาดเล็กน้อย และดีที่สุดตั้งแต่เดือนกุมภาพันธ์  
แต่ด้านลึกยังมีสัญญาณกังวล: การจ้างงานลดลงต่อเนื่อง 2 เดือน, ต้นทุนวัตถุดิบพุ่งสูงสุดในรอบ 6 เดือน และราคาขายพุ่งสูงสุดในรอบ 11 เดือน  
**สาเหตุหลัก** มาจากสงครามตะวันออกกลางที่ทำให้การขนส่งล่าช้า + ผลกระทบจากภาษีศุลกากร  
→ **นัยยะตลาด**: ภาคบริการยังขยายตัว แต่ต้นทุนและราคาสูงขึ้น = สัญญาณเงินเฟ้ออาจไม่ใช่เรื่องชั่วคราว

**อังคาร 7 ก.ค. – ADP Employment Change (ข้อมูลเดือนมิถุนายน)**  
ล่าสุดออกมาแล้ว **+98,000** ตำแหน่งงานภาคเอกชน  
ต่ำกว่าคาด (ตลาดคาด ~110-118k) และลดลงจากเดือนพฤษภาคมที่ +122k  
โดยเฉพาะ Leisure & Hospitality ยังอ่อนแอต่อเนื่อง 6 เดือน  
→ **นัยยะตลาด**: ตลาดแรงงานกำลัง “เย็นลง” ชัดเจน สัญญาณดีต่อการคาดหวัง **Fed ลดดอกเบี้ย** แต่ยังไม่แรงพอที่จะทำให้ Fed เปลี่ยนท่าทีทันที

**พุธ 8 ก.ค. – Fed Meeting Minutes (จากการประชุม 16-17 มิถุนายน)**  
นาทีการประชุมที่ Fed คงดอกเบี้ยที่ระดับ 3.50-3.75%  
เนื้อหาสำคัญที่ตลาดจะจับตามองคือ:
- ความกังวลเรื่องความขัดแย้งในตะวันออกกลางที่กระทบห่วงโซ่อุปทานและราคาพลังงาน
- เงินเฟ้อที่ยังสูงจาก supply shock
- การถกเถียงภายในคณะกรรมการเรื่อง timing การลดดอกเบี้ย
→ **นัยยะตลาด**: ถ้านาทีออกมา “dovish” (เน้นความเสี่ยงเศรษฐกิจ) → ตลาดหุ้นและทองคำดีขึ้น, USD อ่อนค่า  
ถ้า “hawkish” (เน้นเงินเฟ้อจากน้ำมันและภาษี) → USD แข็ง, หุ้นและทองคำกดดัน

**พฤหัส 9 ก.ค. – Initial Jobless Claims + June Existing Home Sales**  
- **Jobless Claims**: ล่าสุดอยู่ที่ 215,000 ราย (ต่ำกว่าคาด) แสดงว่าแรงงานยังไม่เลวร้ายมาก  
- **Existing Home Sales เดือนมิถุนายน**: เดือนพฤษภาคมขายได้ 4.17 ล้านหน่วย (ดีกว่าคาด)  
คาดเดือนมิถุนายนจะอยู่ที่ราว 4.1-4.2 ล้านหน่วย  
แม้ mortgage rate ยังสูง (~6.6%) แต่ inventory เพิ่มขึ้น และผู้ซื้อเริ่มกลับมา  
→ **นัยยะตลาด**: Housing market ฟื้นตัวช้าๆ = สัญญาณเศรษฐกิจไม่แย่ แต่ affordability ยังเป็นปัญหาใหญ่

**ศุกร์ 10 ก.ค. – IEA Monthly Oil Market Report**  
รายงานสำคัญที่สุดของสัปดาห์นี้!  
จากรายงานล่าสุด (มิถุนายน) IEA ปรับลดคาดการณ์ demand น้ำมันโลกปี 2026 ลงเหลือ **ลด 1.1 ล้านบาร์เรล/วัน** เมื่อเทียบปีก่อน  
สาเหตุหลัก: ราคาน้ำมันสูง + disruption จากความขัดแย้งตะวันออกกลาง (โดยเฉพาะเส้นทาง Strait of Hormuz)  
Supply ก็ลดลงหนักเช่นกัน  
→ **นัยยะตลาด**: ถ้ารายงานใหม่ยังยืนยัน demand อ่อนแอ + supply ตึงตัว → ราคาน้ำมันอาจพุ่งต่อ = เงินเฟ้อสูงขึ้น → Fed อาจต้องคงดอกเบี้ยสูงนานขึ้น

**การวิเคราะห์เจาะลึก (สำหรับเทรดเดอร์และนักลงทุน):**

สัปดาห์นี้คือ **สมรภูมิข้อมูล** จริงๆ  
สัญญาณผสมกันชัดเจน:
- **ด้านดี (Dovish)**: ADP อ่อนแอ + Jobless Claims ต่ำ → ตลาดแรงงานเย็นลง = โอกาส Fed ลดดอกเบี้ยในอนาคตเพิ่มขึ้น → Bullish หุ้น, ทองคำ, และสินทรัพย์เสี่ยง
- **ด้านเสี่ยง (Hawkish)**: ราคาน้ำมันและต้นทุนสูงจากสงครามตะวันออกกลาง + ภาษีศุลกากร → เงินเฟ้ออาจ sticky → Fed อาจต้องอดทนถือดอกเบี้ยสูงต่อไป

**สรุปภาพรวม**: ความผันผวน (Volatility) จะสูงมาก โดยเฉพาะวันพุธ (Fed Minutes) และวันศุกร์ (IEA)  
คู่เงิน Forex ที่น่าจับตา: **USDJPY, EURUSD, GBPUSD**  
สินทรัพย์อื่น: ทองคำ (XAUUSD), น้ำมันดิบ (WTI/Brent), S&P 500 และ Nasdaq

**หนึ่งสัปดาห์ก่อน Earnings Season**: ธนาคารใหญ่ (JPMorgan, Bank of America ฯลฯ) จะเริ่มรายงานผล Q2 ช่วงกลางเดือนกรกฎาคมนี้  
ข้อมูลเศรษฐกิจสัปดาห์นี้จะเป็น “ตัวนำ” ที่นักวิเคราะห์และ CEO ใช้เป็นฐานในการให้ guidance  
ถ้าข้อมูลแรงงานอ่อนแอต่อเนื่อง → บริษัทอาจให้ outlook ระมัดระวังมากขึ้น

《7月6日全球财经前瞻：资产逻辑重构与交易机制变革》

全球资产定价逻辑正经历从宏观叙事向业绩与成本精细化管理的切换。美联储7月维持利率不变的概率约为77%，市场高度依赖美国6月服务业PMI及ISM非制造业PMI数据来校验通胀韧性与短端利率路径。黄金与白银价格受避险情绪与利率边际变化驱动，现货黄金触及4200美元/盎司，短期波动率加剧；原油则因8月产油国拟日均增产18.8万桶的供给侧扰动，维持在68.54美元/桶附近的宽幅震荡。宏观层面，全球央行处于通胀回落与政策正常化的磨合期，欧元区投资者信心及零售数据成为观测区域经济韧性的关键窗口。

AI硬件产业链正式从概念炒作期过渡至业绩验证期，三星电子二季度业绩预期及亚洲AI供应链出口数据成为全球科技定价的锚点。尽管硬件链景气度处于上行周期，但随着涨幅扩大，后续资本开支兑现、订单可见度与毛利率表现将替代情绪驱动成为股价核心支撑。与此同时，消费电子领域传来动态，折叠iPhone预期于2026年下半年问世，组装出货量约700万至800万部，带动高端结构件与精密制造链的预期修正。金融科技领域，大模型应用边界正进入严格的合规制度评估期，行业逻辑从技术扩张转向合规安全。

A股市场交易新规于今日正式落地，沪市主板及ETF盘后固定价格交易机制的扩展，显著优化了配置型资金的调仓效率与执行平稳度。数据统计显示，截至6月30日，ETF两融余额增至1160.88亿元，融资资金在半导体、通信等成长主题与黄金ETF防御属性之间构建了双向配置策略。此外，ST板块交易限制的调整标志着监管对存量博弈风险控制的强化。未来市场主线将由单一消息驱动转向“资源股盈利弹性 + 科技链订单确定性 + 制度红利带来的流动性优化”的综合逻辑，投资者需关注杠杆水平与宏观数据再定价带来的溢出效应。

【关键词】：#美联储 #FOMC #服务业PMI #ISM非制造业PMI #全球供应链压力指数 #黄金价格 #原油供给 #AI硬件链 #存储芯片 #三星电子 #光模块 #工业富联 #中际旭创 #新易盛 #A股交易新规 #ETF配置 #两融余额 #半导体 #消费电子 #折叠iPhone #金融科技 #大模型合规 #天问二号 #深空探测 #资源股 #高股息 #资产配置 #波动率 #宏观政策 #利率预期

【观点】：本报告呈现出当前全球资本市场从“宽松预期博弈”向“底层资产与业绩兑现双重回归”的深刻转型。

从激进视角看，市场已进入“去伪存真”的强监管与强验证窗口。AI硬件产业链虽然账面数据靓丽，但7月财报季将成为“分水岭”，缺乏实质订单转化与毛利支撑的标的将面临估值修正，这不仅是财务表现的波动，更是资本市场对AI技术落地深度的终极拷问。保守视角则认为，当前黄金站上4200美元及油价的低开，反映出全球大类资产在防御与波动之间的极度拉扯，地缘政治与宏观不确定性正在降低风险资产的容错率。

中立视角的分析则指出，本次A股交易新规的落地是市场基础设施建设的关键一步，通过引入盘后固定价格交易，实际上是在对冲散户情绪波动，引导资金向ETF等工具型产品流动，这对于市场的长期稳定性至关重要。

核心风险在于“相关性误导”。市场常将宏观降息预期与科技股上涨强相关，但在当前滞涨风险与高利率常态化的双重挤压下，这种因果链条极其脆弱。盲目追高黄金或AI概念，实质上是在忽略“业绩验证窗口”的系统性风险。建议投资者应剥离情绪化表达，聚焦于：1. AI产业链中具备高壁垒、高毛利、确定性资本开支的龙头；2. 具有稳定分红且具备成本护城河的能源与资源品种；3. 随着制度优化，利用ETF工具进行流动性管理和杠杆控制。

当前市场最大的盲点在于，多数投资者过分关注“叙事”，而忽略了“成本”。无论是半导体制造的边际成本，还是金融AI应用的高额合规成本，都将显著削薄未来几年的净利润率。将逻辑抽象至高维，这本质上是全球市场从资本扩张周期转向效率存量周期的缩影。决策的核心应从“交易趋势”转向“验证护城河”，避免在机构博弈的震荡中成为流动性的牺牲品。

你认为在AI硬件进入业绩验证期后，产业链中哪些细分环节具备更强的护城河来抵御毛利率波动？

text:
清晨速递｜全线拉升！高盛重磅研判！美股期指、黄金白银集体走强
 
北京时间7月6日早间，亚太盘初外围市场迎来集体反攻：美股股指期货全线走高，贵金属同步上行；国际油价承压下行，通胀担忧边际缓解。
 
一、美股期指强势反攻，高盛释放战术信号
 
截至7:20，纳指100期货大涨1.45%，标普500期货上涨0.5%，道指期货小幅上涨0.04%。上周五美股因独立日假期休市，而韩国芯片股率先反弹：SK海力士大涨10.88%，三星电子上涨8.22%，对冲此前美股半导体板块大幅回调（费城半导体指数两日累计大跌超11%）。
 
高盛最新交易报告指出，当前已经捕捉到美股动量股初步逢低买盘信号，短线具备战术反弹基础，但风险提示同样明确：
 
1. 动量因子自高点回撤24%，创下2023年一季度以来最大调整，远高于历史均值12%；本轮调整仅10个交易日，而历史平均调整周期约24天。
2. 历史规律显示，2026年以来高贝塔动量板块多次出现两日大跌超10%后修复，当前走势与过往范式相似；本轮下跌更多源于夏季流动性偏弱，AI基本面并未出现根本性逆转，Meta资本开支争议也属于阶段性情绪冲击。

二、贵金属走强

押注霍尔木兹航运恢复，机构预测布油年底最低或跌至60美元

财联社7月4日讯（编辑 赵昊）随着全球石油市场有望重新转向供过于求，一些华尔街策略师开始认为布油价格到今年年底可能跌至每桶60美元，这一水平相当于较中东战事期间的峰值腰斩。

花旗集团分析师在周五的一份报告中表示，尽管局势可能出现短暂波动，但美伊双方都有充分理由维持6月中旬签署的谅解备忘录，石油供应增加将在今年下半年对油价形成下行压力。

花旗建议交易员在今夏油价反弹时逢高卖出，并预测到今年年底，布伦特原油价格将在每桶60至65美元之间。

美伊签署谅解备忘录至今已有两周，但双方围绕备忘录的具体条款和执行依然争论不休

对此，花旗分析师写道，“我们预计谅解备忘录将得到维持，并不是因为双方突然建立了信任，而是因为打破协议对双方都缺乏足够的激励。”

“更广泛的政治背景表明，局势将趋于稳定并最终达成协议，而不是重新走向对抗。”

他们还指出，石油市场的供需基本面仍然“结构性疲软”，“如果产油国决定加快已启动的增产计划，市场供需平衡可能迅速转向明显过剩”。

布伦特原油自2月底以来大幅上涨，随着霍尔木兹海峡被迫关闭航运后进一步飙升。4月30日，布油一度突破每桶126美元，创下2022年以来最高水平。

但随着中东冲突缓和，以及6月美伊签署谅解备忘录后，油价持续回落。截至发稿，布伦特原油报每桶72.13美元。

需要指出的是，花旗预测的油价明显低于市场普遍预期。目前，华尔街对2026年底布油价格的预测中值为78美元，而布油2027年1月交割合约报73美元附近。

上月，高盛将其年底布油价格预测下调至每桶80美元。该行近期报告指出，海湾地区石油运输迅速恢复，80美元的预测基于霍尔木兹海峡的航运流量将在7月底前恢复至战前水平。

高盛也表示，在更乐观的供应正常化情景下，布伦特原油价格到2026年底可能降至每桶60美元左右。

不过，这一预测的前提包括：海湾地区石油出口在“7月初”恢复至战前水平、全球原油日产量比预期高出100万桶以上，以及全球需求弱于预期。

花旗分析师称，目前通过霍尔木兹海峡的原油运输量已回升至每日700万桶。与之相比，冲突爆发前约为每日1500万桶。

他们还指出，实际运输量可能高于官方统计数据，因为出于安全考虑，许多油轮关闭了船舶应答器，因此未被完整纳入统计。

7月4日全球财经晨报：流动性投放、成品油下调、贵金属与AI存储链成为今日主线

【美股】

隔夜美股线索相对有限，全球风险偏好更多由欧洲市场、商品价格与国内政策变量共同定价。

欧洲主要股指集体收涨，德国DAX涨1.00%，显示外盘风险偏好阶段性修复。对A股映射看，港股、恒生科技及高弹性成长方向仍需结合流动性与人民币资产预期观察。

【AI存储链】

存储芯片涨价周期继续强化。

瑞银上调存储价格预期，认为2026年下半年DRAM、NAND价格上行空间扩大，DDR合约价格预期三季度环比上涨32%，NAND三季度环比上涨30%，行业供需紧张或延续至2028年上半年。

产业链消息称，三星第三季度DRAM平均售价拟环比提高约20%。上游涨价将向服务器、PC、手机和消费电子传导，短期利好存储链利润弹性，但也会考验下游客户承受能力。

AI数据中心资本开支正在从算力芯片扩散至内存、PCB、液冷、电源与光模块。SemiAnalysis预计，DRAM、NAND和HBM合计在AI服务器系统中的支出占比，到2026年底将超过30%，2027年或超过40%。

A股映射：兆易创新、北京君正、江波龙、佰维存储、深科技、中际旭创、沪电股份。

【日韩股市】

日韩方向今日核心不在指数本身，而在产业链价格信号。

三星DRAM提价预期强化了全球存储周期上行逻辑，后续重点看韩国存储龙头报价、服务器客户订单、HBM供需以及消费电子端能否顺利消化成本。

对A股而言，存储模组、控制芯片、封测、PCB、服务器电源与液冷链条，都可能继续受益于AI资本开支结构扩散。

【贵金属】

贵金属短期震荡，中长期配置逻辑仍被机构看好。

上海黄金交易所黄金T+D夜盘收涨0.41%，白银T+D收涨0.76%；沪金主力夜盘报913元，涨0.81%；沪银主力夜盘报15294元，涨1.61%。

摩根大通预计黄金短期维持区间震荡，但中长期仍偏乐观，预计2026年三季度黄金均价约4300美元/盎司，四季度约4500美元/盎司。

贵金属主线仍由实际利率、储备配置与避险需求共同定价。短线更适合重视交易纪律，避免在单日波动中追涨杀跌。

A股映射：山东黄金、赤峰黄金、中金黄金、银泰黄金、盛达资源。

【原油】

国内成品油迎年内最大降幅。

国家发改委消息，自7月4日起，国内汽、柴油标准品价格每吨分别下调950元、915元。短期看，物流、航空、化工下游成本压力缓释；上游油气链则需继续关注国际油价、库存与投机持仓变化。

市场快照：SC原油主力夜盘报438元，跌0.16%；布伦特原油9月合约结算价72.12美元。

A股映射：中国国航、顺丰控股、中国石化。

【宏观】

央行流动性投放成为今日最高优先级变量。

公开信息显示，央行将于7月6日开展10000亿元逆回购操作，结束连续3个月缩量。短端资金面改善，有助于缓和季初流动性压力，对高股息、券商、银行及估值弹性板块形成情绪支撑。

但持续性仍要看后续资金价格、信用扩张节奏和实体融资需求。

国内服务业仍具韧性。6月RatingDog中国服务业PMI录得54.1，较5月回落0.3个百分点，但连续第42个月处于扩张区间。服务消费、文旅、电影、线下零售等方向仍有结构性支撑。

【国内线索】

证监会就完善上市公司再融资规则公开征求意见，资本市场制度供给继续向规范融资、提高质量、保护投资者倾斜。短期重点看定增、可转债、并购重组与券商投行业务的边际变化。

三部门调整节能汽车、新能源汽车车船税优惠政策，继续强化汽车消费与绿色交通导向。新能源整车、动力电池、热管理、轻量化零部件仍是政策映射方向。

今年2000亿元“两新”设备更新资金已全部下达，叠加循环经济发展规划，政策组合指向制造业设备更新、节能改造、资源循环利用和再生材料体系。

地方住房消费支持工具继续细化：海南允许每年提取一次住房公积金支付物业费，广州南沙商品住房“以旧换新”补贴7月15日启动。

A股映射：中信证券、华泰证券、比亚迪、宁德时代、三一重工、格林美、中国中免、万达电影、万科A、保利发展。

【科技产业】

英飞凌全球最大功率半导体工厂提前投产，投资额达50亿欧元。新能源车、光伏储能、工业电源与AI数据中心电力系统升级，继续推动功率半导体景气度修复。

A股映射：士兰微、华润微。

有色金属方面，钴出口配额与行政流程扰动提高短期供应不确定性。市场估算最多约2万吨钴存在无法按期发运风险，涉及价值约11亿美元。若现货报价与库存继续共振，钴资源、回收链和三元材料可能出现事件型交易机会。

A股映射：华友钴业、寒锐钴业、格林美、洛阳钼业。

【今日主线】

第一，央行万亿逆回购改善资金面预期，高股息、银行、券商与估值弹性资产受到关注。

第二，国内成品油大幅下调，航空、物流、化工下游成本压力缓释。

第三，钴供应扰动带来有色金属短线弹性，但需警惕流程恢复后的溢价回吐。

第四，黄金和白银中长期逻辑仍在，但短线更适合波段纪律。

第五，存储芯片涨价周期与AI数据中心内存支出占比抬升，是今日科技产业最强主线。

【重点日程】

7月4日：国内汽、柴油价格调整正式执行。

7月6日：央行计划开展10000亿元逆回购操作。

7月7日：CFTC周度持仓报告延后公布。

【一句话总结】

今天的市场关键词是“流动性修复 + 商品分化 + AI存储涨价”：防守端看高股息和贵金属，进攻端看存储芯片、AI基础设施和有色供给扰动。

【风险提示】

商品价格、存储芯片涨价与流动性投放均可能引发短线交易拥挤。

外部不确定性升温可能扰动能源、贵金属和风险资产波动。

请关注价格偏离基本面后的回撤风险，严控杠杆，分散持仓，并设置止损纪律。

仅供研究学习，不构成投资建议。

Market Overview

Global macro desks navigated a highly volatile, holiday-shortened trading frame delivering both the turn of the half-year and massive portfolio rebalancings. Equity index futures arrested their multi-day slide, finding support mid-week as market participants digested a cool ISM Manufacturing PMI print that provided a modest breather for growth multiples. Despite thin liquidity conditions ahead of Friday's Independence Day closure, institutional window-dressing injected sharp positioning shifts across the board.

The dramatic structural divergence within the agricultural complex reached a pivotal juncture. A long time to normalization for US refiners. 

Reason:  three huge forces 

1) Refiners offline only increasing:  Russia, Persian Gulf, Mexico, China - HUGE loss of daily product output 

2) Peak summer demand & throttled inventories of gas, diesel, jet

3) Cheap crude prices.  Market manipulation has pushed refiner key price input low. 

Key:  you only need two of these.  i.e. even if crude rises sharply, products will rise faster as they are more scarce. 

One barrel of crude becomes many things.  The things crude makes become less faster.  Relationship is non-linear.  

See crack spreads during 1973, 79 oil shock analogs.  Crude rises huge - cracks go parabolic as products are more scarce. 

🚨 GLOBAL REFINERY OUTAGE TRACKER

Five regions. Offline simultaneously.

🇷🇺 RUSSIA — 25–50% of national refining capacity DARK. 8 of 10 largest refineries struck. Moscow Kapotnya: offline until 2027. KINEF: fully down since May 5. Diesel seaborne exports collapsed to 426 kb/d — down 50% YoY, lowest since Jan 2017. Gasoline export ban in force.

🛢️ PERSIAN GULF — $58B in damage. 84 facilities struck. ~400–600 kb/d still offline. Kuwait’s three refineries impaired. Bapco (Bahrain): force majeure. UAE Fujairah: hit four times. Pearl GTL (140 kb/d): down. Ras Tanura the only major restart. IEA: 2-year recovery runway.

Salina Cruz (325 kb/d): Diesel hydrotreaters and reformer offline since May 12 — the specific units producing the product most needed right now. Refinería Olmeca, Dos Bocas (340 kb/d): Cogeneration electrical failure June 24. Full emergency shutdown. Smoke column visible for miles. Third major incident at this plant in four months. No credible restart timeline on either facility.

🇺🇸 US EAST COAST — Monroe Energy (Trainer, PA) dark since June 25. No restart timeline. Delta’s 185 kb/d refinery — supplying ~75% of the airline’s total jet fuel via pipeline to NY and Atlanta — taken out by a pump room fire 6 days ago. Delta’s CFO had projected a $300M Q2 benefit from the plant. That hedge just burned. East Coast distillate output hit a 2026 low of 208 kb/d the same week, down 10% WoW. Stocks scraping 23-year lows.

🇨🇳 CHINA — ~3 mb/d offline. Teapot utilization: 72% → 54%. Iranian crude flows down 57%. Teapot volume loss alone exceeds most countries’ entire refining systems

📊 ESTIMATED DAILY PRODUCT LOSSES
🚛 Diesel: ~1.8–2.4 mb/d (~6–8% of global supply) 
⛽ Gasoline: ~1.1–1.4 mb/d (~4–5%) 
✈️ Jet Fuel: ~500–650 kb/d (~7–9%)

⚠️ THE DIESEL CRISIS IS HERE — AND JET FUEL’S LIFELINE IS FRAYING

Russia was the world’s 2nd largest diesel exporter — 11% of global seaborne supply. Russian seaborne diesel exports collapsed to 426 kb/d in June, half of where they were a year ago. June 29: Putin floated a diesel export ban. Backed off. Threat still live. US distillate stocks near 23-year lows. NW Europe diesel stocks down ~20%. The Gulf’s product machine is 2 years from recovery. There is no buffer.

On jet: Asia-Pac has stabilized — but only through demand destruction. Airlines are cutting flights, not filling tanks. Europe is holding via a fragile US export lifeline that has surged from 22 kb/d to 107 kb/d since Hormuz closed — a 5x increase that is now the single pillar keeping European aviation fuel stocks above critical lows. Kpler puts European jet stocks drawing toward 50–55 Mbbls, with a hard floor of 30–35 Mbbls. The margin for error is razor thin.

Monroe Energy going dark June 25 threatens that lifeline at exactly the wrong moment — US summer driving season is already pulling refinery yields toward gasoline and away from jet. Every barrel the US can’t export to Europe is a barrel Europe can’t replace.

One export ban announcement away from a structural diesel crisis. One bad week for US jet exports away from tipping Europe’s aviation fuel market.

Commodities Diverge as Oil Climbs While Metals and Commodity Currencies Lose Momentum | 6th July, 2026
Commodity markets were mixed as resilient WTI crude prices contrasted with weaker gold, silver, and commodity-linked currencies. Oil remained supported despite higher OPEC+ output, while a firmer US Dollar pressured precious metals and weighed on the Australian and Canadian Dollars. Investors now await central bank signals, global demand data, and geopolitical developments for market direction.
https://t.co/xqUtLXuGBw

The oil market is losing its geopolitical premium faster than consensus expected.

OPEC+ key members have agreed on another production hike. The problem: there may be little need for the extra barrels.

That changes the signal.

A few weeks ago, the market was paying for disruption risk. Now it has to price the opposite risk: supply returning into softer demand absorption.

Why it matters: lower oil can ease headline inflation and reduce pressure on central banks. But if the move is driven by excess barrels rather than productivity or strong supply elasticity, it also says something colder about global demand.

Energy bulls need inventories and physical spreads to confirm tightness. Macro investors should watch whether lower crude feeds risk appetite, or whether it starts confirming weaker nominal growth.

The headline is supply. The signal is demand credibility.
